requirements.title: Keyword Namespace Separation
requirements.description: The language separates keywords from ordinary identifiers.
requirements.notes: This avoids collisions between compiler-recognized syntax words and user-defined names.

children.title: Hash-Prefixed Keywords
children.description: Keywords in source code begin with `#` so that words without the prefix remain available as identifiers.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Source code can use a plain word as an identifier even when the corresponding `#`-prefixed form is treated as a keyword.

requirements.title: Builtin Symbol Exposure
requirements.description: The language exposes compiler-provided builtin types and functions through explicit source-level bindings.
requirements.notes: Builtin symbols should follow the same namespace-preserving design direction as `#`-prefixed keywords, so compiler-provided names do not silently occupy ordinary identifiers.

children.title: Explicit Builtin Binding
children.description: Builtin types and functions are made available to user programs only when source code explicitly binds them.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A builtin symbol is not available under an ordinary identifier unless the source code binds it explicitly.
Source code can bind a builtin symbol to an ordinary identifier through a `#`-prefixed builtin-binding construct.

children.title: Standard Library Primitive Exports
children.description: The standard library exposes selected primitive builtin symbols through its public module surface.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public binding for the builtin type `i32`.
The standard library provides public bindings for the builtin arithmetic functions `i32_add`, `i32_sub`, `i32_mul`, `i32_div`, and `i32_mod`.

children.notes: This captures the externally visible standard-library surface without constraining the internal module layout used to organize those bindings.

requirements.title: Package Manifest
requirements.description: Packages and workspaces are described by a manifest file that identifies package metadata, workspace membership, and Felis entrypoints.
requirements.notes: This captures the externally visible manifest contract without constraining unrelated manifest fields or package-specific values.

children.title: Manifest Name And Entrypoint Fields
children.description: A package manifest declares the package name and the Felis library entrypoint.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A package can provide a `neco-package.json` manifest with a `name` field.
A package can provide a `neco-package.json` manifest with a `felis-lib-entrypoint` field.

children.title: Manifest Dependency Fields
children.description: A package manifest can declare dependencies on other packages, including workspace members.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A package can provide a `neco-package.json` manifest with a `dependencies` field.
A package can declare a dependency on another workspace member through a dependency entry with `"workspace": true`.

children.notes: This captures the externally visible dependency declaration shape without constraining future external dependency formats.

children.title: Manifest Binary Entrypoint Fields
children.description: A package manifest can declare one or more Felis binary entrypoint files.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A package can provide a `neco-package.json` manifest with a `felis-bin-entrypoints` field.

children.title: Workspace Member Fields
children.description: A workspace manifest can declare one or more member package directories.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A workspace can provide a `neco-package.json` manifest with a `workspace.members` field.
Each `workspace.members` entry identifies a package directory relative to the workspace root.
Each workspace member directory contains its own `neco-package.json` package manifest.

children.notes: This captures the externally visible workspace-root shape without constraining manifest discovery or command behavior.

children.title: Workspace And Package Separation
children.description: A manifest is either a package manifest or a workspace manifest.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A workspace root manifest can omit package fields such as `name` and Felis entrypoints.
A manifest that declares `workspace` is not required to act as a package.

children.notes: This keeps the workspace model narrow by avoiding a root manifest that is both a workspace and a package.

requirements.title: Module Visibility
requirements.description: Source code can explicitly expose modules and symbols through a module's public surface.
requirements.notes: This captures externally observable module visibility behavior without constraining unrelated name resolution details.

children.title: Public Module Declarations
children.description: Source code can declare a child module as public.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#pub #mod` declaration makes the declared child module part of the enclosing module's public surface.

children.title: Public Re-Exports
children.description: Source code can expose an existing symbol as public from the current module.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#pub #use` declaration makes the referenced symbol part of the current module's public surface.

children.title: Package-Qualified Imports
children.description: Source code can reference a public symbol through a package-qualified path rooted at `#package::`.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#use` declaration can name a public symbol by an absolute package path such as `#package::math::nat::nat::Nat`.

children.notes: This captures the observable import form without constraining how package qualification is resolved internally.

requirements.title: Standard Library Public Surface
requirements.description: The standard library exposes public modules and symbols in addition to primitive builtin bindings.
requirements.notes: This captures the observable standard-library API now present under branches such as `std::io` and `std::math` without constraining unrelated implementation details.

children.title: Natural Number Type
children.description: The standard library provides a public natural-number type with zero and successor constructors.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public type `Nat` under its `math::nat::nat` module.
The public `Nat` type provides public constructors `zero` and `succ`.

children.title: Equality Proposition
children.description: The standard library provides a public equality proposition with a reflexivity constructor.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public proposition `Eq[u]` under its `math::eq::eq` module.
The public `Eq[u]` proposition provides a public constructor `eq_refl`.

children.title: Natural Number Addition
children.description: The standard library provides a public natural-number addition function.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public function `nat_add` under its `math::nat::nat_add` module.

children.title: Natural Number Addition Theorems
children.description: The standard library provides public theorems relating natural-number addition and zero.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public theorem `nat_add_zero_x_eq_x` under its `math::nat::nat_add` module.
The standard library provides a public theorem `nat_add_x_zero_eq_x` under its `math::nat::nat_add` module.

children.title: IO Effect Binding
children.description: The standard library provides a public binding for the builtin `IO` effect.

acceptanceCriteria.description: The standard library provides a public binding `IO` under its `io` module.

children.notes: This captures the observable public binding exposed from `std::io` without constraining the full set of builtin operations available through that effect.

requirements.title: Core Expression And Theorem Forms
requirements.description: Source code supports the expression and theorem forms used by the standard library's documented declarations.
requirements.notes: This captures currently observable source forms without fixing a complete formal grammar for all expressions or proofs.

children.title: Public Function Declarations
children.description: Source code can declare named functions, including public functions exposed from a module.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#fn` declaration can introduce a named function with an explicit type annotation and body.
A `#pub #fn` declaration makes the declared function part of the enclosing module's public surface.

children.title: Public Type Declarations
children.description: Source code can declare named types, including reference-counted public types with constructor lists.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#type` declaration can introduce a named type with a type-level annotation and a body of constructors.
A `#type(rc)` declaration can mark the declared type as reference-counted.
A `#pub #type(rc)` declaration makes the declared type part of the enclosing module's public surface.

children.title: Public Theorem Declarations
children.description: Source code can declare named theorems, including public theorems exposed from a module.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#theorem` declaration can introduce a named theorem with a statement and proof body.
A `#pub #theorem` declaration makes the declared theorem part of the enclosing module's public surface.

children.title: Public Proposition Declarations
children.description: Source code can declare proposition-valued entities, including public propositions with constructor lists.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#prop` declaration can introduce a named proposition with a type annotation.
A proposition declaration body can list constructors.
A `#pub #prop` declaration makes the declared proposition part of the enclosing module's public surface.

children.title: Match Expressions
children.description: Source code can branch by matching a value against cases.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#match` form can inspect a value and provide result expressions for multiple cases.

children.title: Local Let Bindings
children.description: Source code can bind an intermediate value to a local name.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#let` form can bind the result of an expression to a local identifier with an explicit type annotation for subsequent use.

children.title: Universal Quantification In Theorem Statements
children.description: Source code can express universally quantified theorem statements.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A theorem statement can use `#forall` to bind a universally quantified variable together with its type.

children.title: Entrypoint Declarations
children.description: Source code can mark a named function as a binary entrypoint.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A source file can use `#entrypoint <name>;` to identify a function declaration as the program entrypoint.

children.title: Effect-Annotated Function Declarations
children.description: Function declarations can annotate the algebraic effects they perform.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#fn` declaration can place `#with <effect>` between its result type and body.

children.notes: `#with` attaches an Algebraic Effects `Effect` to the function declaration.

children.title: Effectful Let Bindings
children.description: Local let bindings can bind the result of an effectful expression using a left-arrow form.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#let` form can use `<name> : <type> <- <effectful-expression>` to bind the result produced by an effectful expression.

children.title: Let Reference Bindings
children.description: Local reference let bindings can introduce named immutable or exclusive references.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A `#letref` form can use `<name> : & <type> := <value>` to introduce an immutable reference.
A `#letref #excl` form can use `<name> : &^ <type> := <value>` or `<name> : &^ <type> <- <effectful-expression>` to introduce an exclusive reference.

children.notes: The `#excl` marker and the `&^` type annotation must agree.

children.title: String Literal And Method Call Expressions
children.description: Source code supports string literals and the `.>` method-call form used to invoke a method associated with the left-hand side type.

acceptanceCriteria.description: An expression can contain a string literal such as `"Hello, world!"`.
An expression can call a method in the form `<value> .> <method>`.

children.notes: This requirement records only the currently observed surface forms and does not define a full expression grammar. The `.>` form is a method call rather than member access.
